source_title: Single-row vs double-row repair: clinical evidence
topic: Single-row vs double-row repair: clinical evidence
summary: Meta-analyses consistently show double-row (including TOE) repair lowers structural re-tear rates versus single-row, with the benefit concentrated in larger tears (>=3 cm). For small tears (<3 cm), functional outcome scores (ASES, UCLA, Constant) are generally equivalent between single- and double-row. This is a technique-level evidence base that applies across all three vendors' implants - no brand's anchors have been shown in head-to-head RCTs to beat another brand's anchors on clinical outcomes.
arthrex_position: Arthrex markets double-row/TOE SpeedBridge and SutureBridge as the constructs that capture the re-tear-rate advantage shown in the literature, especially for medium-to-large tears.
stryker_position: Stryker positions its double-row platform (ReelX/Omega plus single-row CrossFT/Citrelock options) so surgeons can match construct to tear size, consistent with the size-dependent evidence.
smithnephew_position: Smith & Nephew offers both single-row (HEALICOIL/FOOTPRINT) and knotless double-row HEALICOIL constructs, and additionally frames re-tear reduction around biologic augmentation (REGENETEN) layered on top of either construct.
rep_talk_track: Every rep can honestly cite the same body of meta-analyses: double-row lowers re-tear rates, mostly in larger tears, with similar functional scores to single-row in small tears. The defensible, non-marketing statement is 'the construct evidence favors double-row for bigger tears, and our system executes that construct efficiently' - not 'our anchor heals better than theirs,' which is not supported by head-to-head data.
evidence: Systematic review/meta-analysis (10 studies, 791 patients): double-row had lower re-tear rate (OR ~2.39 favoring DR), better forward-elevation ROM and ASES; for tears <3 cm no significant difference across outcome scores; for tears >=3 cm DR significantly better ASES/UCLA and markedly lower re-tear.
Multiple earlier meta-analyses (e.g., JSES 2014) reach the same directional conclusion: lower re-tear and better cuff integrity with double-row, similar function for small tears.
text:
Single-row vs double-row repair: clinical evidence

Meta-analyses consistently show double-row (including TOE) repair lowers structural re-tear rates versus single-row, with the benefit concentrated in larger tears (>=3 cm). For small tears (<3 cm), functional outcome scores (ASES, UCLA, Constant) are generally equivalent between single- and double-row. This is a technique-level evidence base that applies across all three vendors' implants - no brand's anchors have been shown in head-to-head RCTs to beat another brand's anchors on clinical outcomes.

Arthrex: Arthrex markets double-row/TOE SpeedBridge and SutureBridge as the constructs that capture the re-tear-rate advantage shown in the literature, especially for medium-to-large tears.
Stryker: Stryker positions its double-row platform (ReelX/Omega plus single-row CrossFT/Citrelock options) so surgeons can match construct to tear size, consistent with the size-dependent evidence.
Smith & Nephew: Smith & Nephew offers both single-row (HEALICOIL/FOOTPRINT) and knotless double-row HEALICOIL constructs, and additionally frames re-tear reduction around biologic augmentation (REGENETEN) layered on top of either construct.

Rep positioning: Every rep can honestly cite the same body of meta-analyses: double-row lowers re-tear rates, mostly in larger tears, with similar functional scores to single-row in small tears. The defensible, non-marketing statement is 'the construct evidence favors double-row for bigger tears, and our system executes that construct efficiently' - not 'our anchor heals better than theirs,' which is not supported by head-to-head data.

Evidence: (1) Systematic review/meta-analysis (10 studies, 791 patients): double-row had lower re-tear rate (OR ~2.39 favoring DR), better forward-elevation ROM and ASES; for tears <3 cm no significant difference across outcome scores; for tears >=3 cm DR significantly better ASES/UCLA and markedly lower re-tear. (2) Multiple earlier meta-analyses (e.g., JSES 2014) reach the same directional conclusion: lower re-tear and better cuff integrity with double-row, similar function for small tears.
